name: Góngora Trail to Ermita de la Sierra
name_original: Subida a la Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Sierra desde Cabra — circular por el Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas
description: This difficult 7.8-mile (12.5 km) circular jogging route ascends 2146 feet (654 metres) to the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Sierra.
region_intro: Jogging around Cabra offers diverse landscapes and features suitable for outdoor activities. The region is situated within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, providing a backdrop of natural beauty and varied terrain. Runners can explore routes that traverse olive groves, historic greenways, and paths with significant elevation changes, catering to different fitness levels. The area's physical features include converted railway lines like the Vía Verde del Aceite and hills leading to viewpoints. Best jogging routes around Cabra The most popular jogging route is Góngora Trail to Ermita de la Sierra, a 7.8 miles (12.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 52 minutes to complete. This difficult route includes a substantial ascent, offering panoramic views of the surrounding area. Another top favourite among local runners is Chorreras de la Fuenseca y Poljé de la Nava de Cabra — circular por el Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas, a moderate 8.2 miles (13.2 km) path. This route explores the natural park, featuring varied terrain and natural formations. Local runners also love the Olive Oil Greenway, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through olive groves, often completed in about 30 minutes. Jogging around Cabra is defined by historic greenways, olive groves, and the hills of the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy, paved paths to more challenging routes with elevation gains. The routes in Cabra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 300 runners have used komoot to explore Cabra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners in Cabra?
A: Yes, Cabra offers several easy running routes perfect for beginners. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1286046953/olive-oil-greenway" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olive Oil Greenway&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.1 miles (5.0 km) long and leads through picturesque olive groves on mostly paved surfaces. This route is generally completed in about 30 minutes and has minimal elevation gain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails in Cabra?
A: The running trails in Cabra offer a diverse range of terrain. You can find easy, paved paths like sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite, which is ideal for a smooth run. Other routes, especially those within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, feature more varied terrain with significant elevation changes, including dirt paths and ascents to viewpoints.

Q: Are there challenging running routes for experienced runners in Cabra?
A: Absolutely. For experienced runners seeking a challenge, Cabra provides routes with substantial ascents and longer distances. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1286818824/gongora-trail-to-ermita-de-la-sierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Góngora Trail to Ermita de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 7.8-mile (12.5 km) route with significant elevation gain, offering panoramic views. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001056758/hermitage-of-calvario-and-sima-de-cabra-circular-from-cabra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Calvario and Sima de Cabra – circular from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;, a 7.4-mile (11.9 km) difficult path.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Cabra?
A: Yes, many of Cabra's running routes are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1286825222/chorreras-de-la-fuenseca-y-polje-de-la-nava-de-cabra-circular-por-el-parque-natural-sierra-subbeticas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorreras de la Fuenseca y Poljé de la Nava de Cabra — circular por el Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 8.2-mile (13.2 km) circular path exploring the natural park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001056758/hermitage-of-calvario-and-sima-de-cabra-circular-from-cabra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Calvario and Sima de Cabra&lt;/a&gt; also offers a challenging circular experience.

Q: What scenic views or landmarks can I see while jogging in Cabra?
A: Cabra's running routes offer stunning scenic views and historical landmarks. Along the Vía Verde del Aceite, you can enjoy views of the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4741710" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alamedal Viaduct&lt;/a&gt;. Routes like the Góngora Trail lead to the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Sierra, providing panoramic vistas. While not directly on a running route, the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zuheros Castle&lt;/a&gt; can be admired from certain viewpoints in the region.

Q: Are the running routes in Cabra suitable for families?
A: Many running routes in Cabra are suitable for families, especially those looking for an easier, more accessible experience. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1286046953/olive-oil-greenway" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olive Oil Greenway&lt;/a&gt;, with its paved surfaces and gentle gradients, is an excellent choice for families to enjoy a run or walk together amidst olive groves.

Q: Are there dog-friendly running options in Cabra?
A: While specific dog regulations can vary, many of the natural trails and greenways in Cabra, particularly within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, are generally welcoming to dogs, provided they are kept on a leash and owners clean up after them. Always check local signage for any specific restrictions on individual routes or protected areas.

Q: How many running routes are available in Cabra?
A: There are over 30 dedicated running routes around Cabra, catering to various fitness levels and preferences. This includes 3 easy routes, 20 moderate routes, and 13 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for everyone.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Cabra?
A: The running routes in Cabra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 5 reviews. Runners particularly enjoy the diverse landscapes, from serene olive groves to challenging trails with panoramic views, and the well-maintained greenways that offer a pleasant experience.

Q: Are there any routes that combine running with historical sites?
A: Yes, some routes in Cabra offer a blend of running and historical exploration. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001056758/hermitage-of-calvario-and-sima-de-cabra-circular-from-cabra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Calvario and Sima de Cabra&lt;/a&gt; route, for instance, combines scenic vistas with historical sites, providing a culturally enriching running experience.

Q: Can I access running trails in Cabra using public transport?
A: While specific public transport links directly to every trailhead might be limited, Cabra itself is accessible by public transport. From the town, you can often reach the starting points of many routes, such as the Vía Verde del Aceite, which passes through the area. For routes deeper in the natural park, a short drive from Cabra might be necessary.

Q: Are there running routes with good parking facilities in Cabra?
A: Many of the popular running routes around Cabra, especially those starting from the town or along the Vía Verde del Aceite, offer convenient parking options nearby. It's advisable to check the specific route details on komoot for information on parking availability at the trailhead.
tips: This hermitage is located at the top of the Picacho de la Sierra de Cabra. From the height of 1200 masl you have spectacular views of the mountains and the countryside.
Another railway station that has been converted into a restaurant. A great area to stop and take some photos of the old steam locomotives that used to circulate on this route.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult. It involves a significant ascent of over 650 meters to the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Sierra, with sections featuring steep gradients between 6% and 10% or more, particularly on the fourteen switchbacks leading to the hermitage. It requires good fitness and sure-footedness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and challenging. You'll encounter pedestrian-only sections, such as 'Las Cruces' near the sanctuary, as well as roads with traffic. The path includes rugged sections with rocks, especially as you gain elevation, and steep, winding ascents. Some parts traverse the beautiful karst formations of the Parque Natural de las Sierras Subbéticas.

faqs.question: Are there any specific sections or features I should be aware of?
faqs.answer: Yes, be aware of 'Las Cruces,' a pedestrian-only stretch between the access road and the sanctuary where non-pedestrian transit is prohibited. The final approach to the hermitage involves narrow roads with fourteen switchbacks and consistently steep gradients. Also, much of the ascent offers limited shade until the final kilometer.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The primary destination is the historic Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra, located on El Picacho, also known as the 'Balcón de Andalucía,' offering stunning panoramic views. The route also passes through areas with ancient oak trees and opportunities to see purebred Andalusian horses, particularly through the La Viñuela estate. The starting point is near Cabra Station.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with several other official trails, including the Camino Mozárabe desde Málaga, the Via Verde del Aceite, the [CIMA CO03] Virgen de la Sierra, and the Camino de Góngora.

faqs.question: Is this jogging route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas generally allows dogs, it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially given the presence of wildlife and potential livestock. Be mindful of the challenging terrain and limited shade, which might be difficult for some dogs, particularly during warmer months.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas?
faqs.answer: Generally, there is no entrance fee or specific permit required for accessing the trails within the Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas, and ensure you leave no trace.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to tackle this route. The temperatures are milder, making the significant ascent more manageable. Summers can be very hot with limited shade, and winters can bring colder conditions at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Where can I park near the trailhead in Cabra?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Cabra, near the Cabra Station. You should be able to find parking within the town of Cabra, though specific trailhead parking might require a short walk to the official start point.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: For jogging, the estimated duration for this 12.5 km route with over 650 meters of elevation gain is approximately 1 hour and 52 minutes. However, due to its 'difficult' classification and steep sections, individual times may vary significantly based on fitness levels and pace.

faqs.question: What are the views like from the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Sierra?
faqs.answer: The views from the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra, located on El Picacho (the 'Balcón de Andalucía'), are a major highlight. You can expect extensive, breathtaking panoramic vistas across the surrounding Parque Natural Sierra Subbéticas and beyond, making the challenging ascent well worth the effort.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra
